• Analysis: Eigenvalue distributions and ablations on covariance rank convincingly illustrate the expressiveness gain.  

Weaknesses:
• Clarity: The scaled energy temperature tuning procedure and its sensitivity to dataset characteristics require more discussion.  
• Complexity: Inference costs grow with covariance rank; scalability to very high-dimensional embeddings or many classes is unclear.  
• Ablation: More experiments on rank selection and alternative set encoders (e.g. DeepSets) would strengthen claims.  

Questions and suggestions:
1. How robust is calibration to the choice of initial temperature and tuning schedule?  
2. Can the framework handle tasks with varying class counts without retraining?  
3. Please report memory/time overheads for higher rank settings and typical backbones.  
4. Minor: clarify notation in Eq. 10 and append pseudocode for temperature tuning.  
Overall, this is a solid contribution with clear gains in meta-uncertainty estimation.","summary_of_the_review":"The paper introduces a meta-learning method to predict class-wise diagonal and low-rank covariance matrices via a set encoder and a scaled energy softmax to improve calibration under distribution shift. It demonstrates significant OOD calibration gains in few-shot benchmarks. Clarity on temperature tuning, runtime for high ranks, and additional ablations would improve the presentation.","recommendation":"8: accept, good submission"}

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper addresses the challenge of estimating class-conditional feature covariances in meta-learning for improved deterministic uncertainty quantification and OOD calibration. The authors identify that empirical covariance estimation fails in few-shot settings and propose a meta-learned covariance generator based on an attentive set encoder that outputs either diagonal or diagonal-plus-low-rank factors. They further introduce an energy-based inference procedure that parameterizes a logit-normal softmax with variance scaled by the minimum energy, mitigating overconfidence from softmax shift invariance. Bi-Lipschitz regularization via spectral normalization preserves feature distances. Experiments on synthetic toy tasks, Omniglot, MiniImageNet and their corrupted variants demonstrate superior OOD calibration (ECE) while maintaining competitive in-distribution accuracy.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths: The paper identifies a clear limitation of existing covariance-based uncertainty methods in meta-learning and proposes a principled solution combining meta-learned low-rank covariance factors and energy-based softmax calibration. The use of a set transformer as a covariance encoder is well motivated by the need to capture pairwise feature interactions. The recursive inversion and determinant lemmas yield efficient inference, and bi-Lipschitz regularization ensures expressive, non-collapsed features. Extensive experiments on toy and standard few-shot benchmarks show significant ECE improvements under OOD shifts.

Weaknesses and Suggestions: 1) Baselines: Important recent meta-learning uncertainty methods such as deep kernel transfer (Patacchiola et al., 2020) are only briefly mentioned. A direct quantitative comparison would strengthen claims of novelty and performance. 2) Ablation on rank: While rank-1 and rank-2 variants are evaluated, the effect of varying the low-rank dimension and its impact on calibration vs. computation is not fully explored. 3) Clarity: The description of the logit-normal approximation and temperature tuning is dense; a clearer step-by-step derivation and pseudocode would aid reproducibility. 4) OOD evaluation: Corruptions and random novel classes are useful but limited; consider evaluating on standard OOD benchmarks (e.g., CIFAR-10 vs. SVHN) to assess generalization. 5) Implementation details: More detail on hyperparameter choices, training stability and set encoder architecture (number of blocks, heads) would help replication. 6) Computational cost: Although low-rank inference is efficient, experiments show latency increases with rank; discussing tradeoffs in practical deployments would be beneficial.

Overall, the approach is sound, original and significant for meta-learning uncertainty. Addressing the above points in revision will improve clarity and impact.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper presents a novel meta-learning framework that predicts diagonal and low-rank covariance factors via a set transformer and employs energy-based softmax calibration, yielding improved OOD calibration in few-shot settings. Experiments demonstrate strong ECE gains, though comparisons to recent baselines, detailed ablations on rank choice, clearer derivations, broader OOD tests,and more implementation detail are needed for full reproducibility and assessment.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper addresses uncertainty quantification in few-shot and out-of-distribution (OOD) scenarios by meta-learning low-rank covariance factors for class-conditional Gaussian models in embedding space. Building on bi-Lipschitz regularized feature extractors, the authors train a set encoder that produces either diagonal or diagonal plus low-rank covariance matrices per task. They also introduce an energy-based inference procedure with scaled logit-normal variance to mitigate the shift-invariance overconfidence of softmax. Experiments on synthetic datasets, Omniglot, and MiniImageNet (including corruption benchmarks) demonstrate improved calibration and competitive accuracy compared to Prototypical Networks, SNGP, DDU, MAML, and Reptile.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths:
• Novel combination of meta-learning and low-rank covariance prediction to yield expressive class-conditional distributions even in few-shot settings.
• Energy-based scaling elegantly addresses softmax shift invariance, improving OOD calibration in benchmarks.
• Comprehensive synthetic and real-world experiments, including corruption intensities and OOD class detection, show consistent ECE and NLL gains.

Weaknesses:
• Theoretical justification for the set encoder’s ability to generalize covariance structure across tasks is limited; no bounds or guarantees are provided.
• Sensitivity to rank hyperparameter is not fully analyzed; only a few ranks are evaluated without clear guidance for selection.
• Computational complexity increases quickly with rank; runtime tables do not include large-scale or higher-dimensional tasks.
• Notational inconsistencies in Section 3.3 hinder readability, and some figures lack axis labels or clear captions.
• Baseline comparisons omit Bayesian meta-posterior approaches (e.g., deep kernel transfer) and modern normalizing flows for uncertainty.

Questions:
1. How robust is performance to the choice of rank and encoder architecture? Any failure modes when support sets are unbalanced?
2. Can the energy temperature tuning procedure overfit to validation distributions? How stable is it across shifts?

Suggestions:
• Provide ablation on encoder depth, attention heads, and hyperparameter sensitivity.
• Improve clarity of equations and consistently label all figures/tables.
• Discuss memory/runtime trade-offs for high-rank inference and potential approximations.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper proposes a meta-learning framework for predicting low-rank covariance factors and an energy-based inference for calibrated OOD detection. Empirical results are strong, but theoretical motivation, hyperparameter analysis, scalability, notation clarity, and broader baseline comparisons require improvement before acceptance.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper introduces Proto Mahalanobis, a meta‐learning approach for constructing task‐specific class covariance matrices in few‐shot settings. Rather than relying on empirical estimates, the method uses an attentive set encoder to predict either diagonal or diagonal plus low‐rank factors for each class covariance, enabling rich covariance structure with limited support data. To address softmax shift invariance and improve calibration under distributional shifts, the authors propose a scaled energy–based logit‐normal inference procedure. Bi‐Lipschitz regularization via spectral normalization is employed to preserve feature distances. Experiments on synthetic toy tasks, Omniglot and MiniImageNet (including corrupted variants) demonstrate improved expected calibration error (ECE) and competitive classification accuracy compared to prototypical networks, SNGP, DDU and gradient‐based meta‐learners.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths
• Novelty: The paper identifies limitations of empirical covariances in meta‐learning and presents a principled set‐encoder solution for low‐rank covariance prediction.
• Calibration: The scaled energy–based inference addresses overconfidence of softmax under OOD shifts and yields consistently lower ECE across tasks.
• Experimental scope: Results on toy datasets, Omniglot‐C and MiniImageNet‐C cover several corruption intensities and compare against relevant baselines.
• Methodological rigor: Use of spectral normalization enforces bi‐Lipschitz regularity, and efficient inversion formulas reduce cubic overhead.

Weaknesses
• Energy scaling heuristic: The temperature tuning procedure (increment until a calibration criterion) is ad hoc and may not generalize; more principled guidance or ablation is needed.
• Rank selection: The choice of low‐rank dimension r is not justified; sensitivity analysis over r and its impact on overfitting or underfitting is missing.
• Limited theoretical support: There is no formal analysis of why the set encoder captures covariance structure beyond empirical observation.
• Computational cost: Although inversion uses low‐rank formulas, runtime overhead relative to simple prototypes should be quantified on larger tasks.
• Dataset diversity: Experiments focus on few‐shot benchmarks; performance on larger or real‐world shifts (e.g. adversarial or natural domain shifts) is untested.

Questions and Suggestions
1. How sensitive is calibration performance to the energy‐scaling function h(E) and the thresholding constant? Can a continuous relaxation or learned mapping replace the heuristic?
2. Provide ablations that isolate the benefits of the low‐rank factors versus purely diagonal covariances and versus increasing support set size.
3. Evaluate OOD detection metrics such as AUROC/AUPR alongside ECE to assess separation between ID and OOD.
4. Include runtime and memory profiling on a larger backbone (e.g. ResNet) to demonstrate scalability.
5. Discuss potential failure modes when tasks exhibit highly anisotropic or multimodal class distributions.

Overall, the paper presents a compelling advance in meta‐learning covariance for uncertainty quantification, but would benefit from deeper analysis of design choices and broader empirical validation.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper proposes meta‐learning low‐rank class covariances via a set transformer and an energy‐based inference to improve calibration in few‐shot tasks. Strong empirical gains in ECE are shown, but the work relies on heuristic temperature tuning, lacks rank‐selection analysis and theoretical insight, and is tested only on small benchmarks. Further ablations, principled justification of hyperparameters and runtime evaluation on larger models are needed.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents an end-to-end differentiable physics framework for learning complex locomotion skills in soft agents via unified neural network controllers. The authors integrate periodic activation functions, tailored loss terms for running, jumping and crawling, batching of simulation instances and a modified Adam optimizer to enhance robustness and efficiency. They implement both mass–spring and material point method (MPM) simulators in a differentiable Taichi-based environment, and conduct extensive ablation studies to quantify the impact of each component. Empirical comparisons on multiple 2D and 3D soft-robot designs demonstrate faster convergence and lower validation loss than a PPO-based reinforcement learning baseline. The learned controllers can smoothly interpolate among velocity, height and orientation goals and support interactive real-time control.
reviews.main_review: Strengths: The paper addresses an important challenge of leveraging differentiable physics for multi-skill locomotion, moving beyond single-goal controllers. The combination of SIREN-style sinusoidal activations, period-aware loss functions and batching yields clear empirical gains and stable gradient behavior over hundreds of time steps. The ablation studies are comprehensive, isolating the contributions of optimizer hyperparameters, activation functions, input feature subsets and loss design. Comparisons to PPO further highlight the efficiency and performance advantages of gradient-based training in this domain. The interactive control demonstration and 3D experiments extend the paper’s practical relevance. Weaknesses: Evaluation remains confined to synthetic simulation tasks without a real-robot or hardware-in-the-loop validation. The framework’s computational cost, memory requirements and sensitivity to simulator accuracy are not fully quantified. While PPO is a reasonable baseline, additional comparisons to model-based RL or other differentiable simulators would strengthen the empirical claims. Questions and suggestions: How does performance scale with increased task complexity or longer simulation horizons? Can the method handle contact-rich behaviors such as climbing or manipulation beyond periodic locomotion? A discussion of failure modes and guidelines for robot design would be valuable. More detail on solver performance and GPU utilization would aid reproducibility. Overall, the paper offers a significant advance in differentiable physics learning for soft robotics, with solid experimental validation and insightful analysis.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper introduces a unified differentiable physics framework for multi-skill soft-robot locomotion, combining sinusoidal activations, tailored losses, batching and a modified Adam optimizer. Extensive ablations and PPO comparisons demonstrate faster convergence and robustness, though evaluation is limited to simulation and computation costs are not fully detailed.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents an end-to-end differentiable physics learning framework for training a single neural network controller to perform multiple locomotion skills on soft-body agents. Building on mass-spring and moving least squares material point method simulators, the authors introduce a suite of improvements—including periodic SIREN activations, tailored loss functions, batched simulation, and a modified Adam optimizer—that together enable unified controllers to accomplish running, jumping, crawling (2D) and running plus rotation (3D) with user‐specified velocity, height, and orientation targets. Extensive ablation studies isolate the contribution of each component, and comparisons against PPO demonstrate significantly faster convergence and lower validation loss. The system also supports interactive real‐time control and smooth interpolation among skills.
reviews.main_review:
Strengths: The work addresses a clear gap by demonstrating that differentiable physics can scale beyond simple single-goal tasks to support multiple, continuous locomotion skills with a single network. The paper offers a comprehensive ablation study isolating optimizer choice, activation functions, loss design, input features, and batch size, which substantiates each design decision. The experiments cover multiple simulation backends, agent geometries, motion tasks, and include interactive demonstrations. The modified Adam hyperparameters and periodic activations are well motivated by gradient‐stability and periodicity considerations. Comparison to PPO provides a baseline for data‐efficiency and convergence improvement.

Weaknesses and Suggestions:
1. Baselines: The paper compares only to PPO. It would strengthen the contribution to include comparisons to existing differentiable physics controllers (e.g. ChainQueen) on comparable tasks or to state‐of‐the‐art soft‐robot learning methods.  
2. Computational cost and scalability: Details on wall‐clock training time, GPU resources, and per‐iteration cost are missing. Reporting throughput (steps/sec) would aid reproducibility and adoption.  
3. Robustness: All experiments are in simulation with idealized dynamics. How sensitive is the controller to model error or perturbations? A noise or domain randomization study would add credibility for real‐world soft robotics.  
4. Presentation: Several typos and formula formatting issues distract (e.g., “dfferentible,” inconsistent notation). Loss definitions in Section 3.2 would benefit from clearer equation numbering and prose explanation.  
5. Hyperparameter sensitivity: The choice of SIREN activation and loss weights seems critical—provide guidance or heuristics for tuning these in new tasks.  

Overall, the paper makes a significant step in scaling differentiable physics to complex multi‐skill locomotion, and with additional baseline comparisons and more detailed reporting it would be a strong contribution.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper offers a novel framework for multi‐skill locomotion controller training via differentiable physics, supported by extensive ablations and interactive demos. It convincingly shows faster convergence than PPO, but would be strengthened by additional baseline comparisons to other differentiable physics methods, detailed computational cost reporting, robustness evaluations, and improved presentation of loss definitions and hyperparameter choices.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: This paper presents a differentiable physics–based framework for training unified neural network controllers capable of complex soft‐robot locomotion skills. The authors introduce a suite of enhancements to existing approaches, including periodic activation functions, tailored loss functions for running, jumping, and crawling, a batched simulator execution, and a modified Adam optimizer with reduced momentum. The system supports mass–spring and material point method simulations, various agent morphologies, and multiple goals with continuous interpolation of velocity, height, and orientation. Extensive ablation studies demonstrate the contribution of each component, and comparisons against PPO show faster convergence and greater flexibility. The framework also enables real‐time interactive control of soft agents.
reviews.main_review: Strengths: The paper tackles a challenging problem of multi‐skill soft locomotion via differentiable simulation. The proposed enhancements are well motivated, and the ablation studies systematically isolate their impacts. The comparison to PPO illustrates clear efficiency gains. The interactive control demonstration is compelling, and the framework supports multiple simulation backends and robot designs. Weaknesses: Numerous minor flaws detract from the overall clarity and reproducibility. Equation 4 contains stray symbols and misaligned indices; notation for summations and goals is inconsistent. Table 1 and several figure captions lack clear references and units. The choice and tuning of loss weights (λv, λh, λc) and Adam hyperparameters are only briefly described; sensitivity to these values is unclear. Friction and damping models are discussed in appendices but no quantitative analysis of their effect is given in the main text. Runtime metrics (wall‐clock time, memory usage) are omitted. The PPO baseline is not fully tuned (e.g., no SAC or TRPO comparison), and statistical significance beyond mean±std is missing. Questions: How sensitive is performance to loss‐weight selection? How does the framework handle contact discontinuities or noise? Can it scale to more complex 3D bodies or longer horizons? Suggestions: Correct formatting and typos in equations, unify notation, report absolute performance metrics (distance, energy), include more baselines, and clarify hyperparameter choices in the main paper.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper offers a novel differentiable physics approach with strong empirical ablations and interactive demos, but suffers from numerous minor methodological and presentation issues—ambiguous notation, missing runtime metrics, limited baselines, and unclear hyperparameter selection—that hinder clarity and reproducibility.

reviews.summary_of_the_paper: The manuscript proposes a differentiable physics-based learning framework to train unified neural network controllers for complex soft-agent locomotion tasks. It integrates periodic activation functions, tailored loss designs, batch processing, and a modified Adam optimizer for improved efficiency and robustness. Evaluated on mass-spring and MLS-MPM simulations across multiple soft-body designs, the system supports concurrent tasks—running, jumping, crawling, and turning—with controllable velocity, height, and orientation. Comprehensive ablations highlight the contributions of each component, and comparisons against PPO demonstrate faster convergence and lower validation losses. Interactive control of trained agents is showcased, and the framework claims to be the first to achieve multiple locomotion skills on soft agents with a single network.
reviews.main_review: This paper addresses an important challenge: training neural controllers for rich soft-body behaviors via differentiable simulation. Its strengths lie in the systematic ablation studies, end-to-end gradient evaluation, and clear comparisons to PPO. The tailored loss functions and periodic signals are well motivated and supported by results. The batched differentiable simulator and modified Adam show significant speedups. However, the work remains confined to simulation; there is no real or noisy hardware validation to assess reality gap or robustness to unmodeled dynamics. The scalability to more complex morphologies or longer horizons is unclear. Hyperparameter sensitivity, choice of physical parameters, and stability under perturbations merit deeper discussion. Figures and tables are dense and could be streamlined. I suggest clarifying assumptions on material properties, elaborating on failure cases, and releasing code to strengthen reproducibility. Overall, the methodology is sound and results are promising, but the empirical scope should be broadened to justify broader claims.
reviews.summary_of_the_review: The paper presents a solid differentiable physics learning framework with thorough ablations and convincing simulation results. Key innovations include periodic activations, tailored losses, batching, and a modified optimizer. Missing real-world validation and discussions on scalability limit the impact. Methodologically sound but scope should be extended.

However, the work remains confined to simulation; there is no real or noisy hardware validation to assess reality gap or robustness to unmodeled dynamics. The scalability to more complex morphologies or longer horizons is unclear. Hyperparameter sensitivity, choice of physical parameters, and stability under perturbations merit deeper discussion. Figures and tables are dense and could be streamlined. I suggest clarifying assumptions on material properties, elaborating on failure cases, and releasing code to strengthen reproducibility.
